The Official BNI Podcast

A weekly audio discussion with Dr. Ivan Misner, the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, the world's largest business networking organization.

Episode 250: When Is Two Not Better Than One?

April 4, 2012 by Ivan Misner 27 Comments

Podcast: Download

Subscribe: Apple Podcasts | RSS | Subscription Options

Print This Episode

Synopsis

A member recently asked Dr. Misner why you can’t belong to two BNI chapters or two groups like BNI.

This is a more important issue than people realize at first.

In 1985 and 1986 BNI allowed members to belong to two groups. It was a DISASTER. The Board of Advisors received floods of complaints from members. The divided loyalties of members who belonged to more than one group created serious trust issues in both chapters.

A few years later, a member asked why BNI members were allowed to belong to organizations that competed with BNI (other referral networks) if they weren’t allowed to belong to two BNI groups, since that created the same problem of split loyalties.

The only person who benefits from being in two groups is that individual member. The other members of the groups benefit more from having all their members fully committed to them.

If you’re not sure that belonging to competing groups is a bad idea, the next time you’re at your BNI meeting, look to your left. Look to your right. Imagine those people are in another BNI chapter with your profession represented in it. Who is going to get the referrals?

Leave a comment and tell us how you would feel in this situation.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 250

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing great, Priscilla, and I have an interesting topic.

Priscilla:
Okay. Tell us about it.

Ivan:
When is two not better than one? Two of everything is always better, right?

Priscilla:
More is better, right?

Ivan:
Well, not on this topic. I was recently asked a question by a BNI member as to why we can’t belong to BNI chapters or why we can’t belong to two groups like BNI. Here is sort of an edited version of my response. I hope this is of value to BNI members as to why this policy exists. There is really a long history relating to this issue, and it’s important to frame the reason why this is such an important issue. The reason for framing it is that people don’t really care about the how until they understand why. Once they understand why it makes more sense as to how to do something.

Here is why you can’t be in two BNI groups or two groups like BNI: In 1985 and 1986, we actually allowed members of BNI to belong to two BNI chapters. Did you know that?

Priscilla:
No, I had no idea.

Ivan:
You could. You could belong to two groups. It was a disaster. It was a huge disaster. There were so many complains that when I created the board of advisers in 1986- remember this is created of BNI members- one of the first things they did was vote on this policy that I had created that you could belong to two BNI chapters. They thought it created animosity among members because a BNI member was splitting his or her loyalty between the two BNI groups. Back then, there were more complaints among members on this one issue than any other issue in the organization, including attendance.

Priscilla:
That makes sense.

Ivan:
In retrospect, everything is 20/20. When I look at this, I think that was a really dumb idea that I had. There were some stipulations. They couldn’t be side by side. They had to be a significant distance from each other, but it was really all irrelevant. It all came down to split loyalties. The answer seemed really obvious to the board of advisers. Allowing someone to belong to two BNI chapters split their loyalties and created serious trust issues in the affected group. So one of the very first policies that the board created was that you can’t belong to two BNI groups.

Now fast forward a few years. In the early 1990’s the board of advisers met to address a complaint from a BNI member on a somewhat related issue. Here is a quote of what he said to me, “If BNI doesn’t allow someone to be a member of two BNI groups, why in the world would BNI allow a member to belong to one BNI group and another group just like BNI but that competes with BNI? What’s the difference and why would we allow it? Isn’t it the same thing as allowing somebody to be in two BNI groups?

I thought, wow, that is an interesting comment. So I presented that to the board of advisers. They heard the argument and discussed it for a very long time. In the end, they completely agreed. That night, in my home in the early ’90s- that was the first office for BNI and we ran BNI out of my house. Now BNI is in a 33,000 sq ft building, but back then, BNI was run out of a couple spare bedrooms of my house.

The board of advisers decided that we should create a policy that a BNI member could not belong to two BNI groups and couldn’t belong to two strong contact groups like BNI for the same reason that you can’t belong to two BNI groups. They felt it would split the loyalties to belong to two groups like BNI. They felt that if we don’t allow members to be in two BNI groups, it makes even less sense to allow them to be in other groups just like BNI. This was a decision made by the board of advisers, members of BNI, back in the early ’90s.

One of the main arguments that was made by the board of advisers on this issue was that the person who benefits most from this arrangement of being in two groups is only the person who is in two groups. The other people in those groups would benefit most by having someone who is dedicated to them and not splitting their commitment. Does that makes sense?

It was a really tough discussion. Again, these were members who made this decision. They really felt is was best for the entire group because they really felt if we let people belong to multiple BNI chapters or groups like BNI, it weakens the loyalty in good groups made up of committed members and it will take a potentially strong chapter and make it mediocre.

Here is my guiding principle on that concept of making something mediocre. Why let someone talk you into mediocrity when excellence is an option? Don’t let a few people determine the success of the whole chapter. Stand tall as a group and make the decision to have a better group. Don’t let people talk you into being in two groups of BNI or two groups like BNI.

It was policy number 16 that exists today that addresses this issue now. It says that membership committees have authority in this area. Here is something really interesting, Priscilla, somebody recently asked me, “Okay, so membership committees have authority. Can they ignore it?” The short answer is the definitive answer. No. They can’t ignore it.

The police have full authority when someone robs a bank. Does this mean it is okay for them to ignore it? No, it means they are charged with enforcing the law.

So policy number 16 as written currently by the board of advisers means that the membership committee is charged with doing the right thing and enforcing the rule within the group, not ignoring the rule. Remember, you can never go wrong by doing the right thing. The right thing in a strong contact networking group like BNI is expecting support by the people that are in the room. It is obvious that being in two BNI groups is a bad idea. We have history that bears that out. It is no different from being in two groups like BNI. If you expect excellence, commitment, and accountability you can achieve success. If you allow yourself to be talked into mediocrity, you then strive to be average.

The way this often happens is that people try to talk other members of the group into thinking it’s okay. It’s no big deal. They are in this other group and it’s fine. But that has a big impact on the group. It’s not easy to stand up for what is in the best interest of a trusting group of referral partners. But that is what chapters need to do in order to achieve success. Share with members why this type of loyalty within a BNI group is so important. I would urge people in chapters who are listening to this podcast to share it with other members.

If you are listening to it and are not sure that this is a good idea and think that it’s a bad idea and you should be in other groups just like BNI, the next time that you are in your BNI meeting, look to your left and look to your right. Imagine that those people in your BNI meeting are in another BNI chapter and those groups, other BNI chapters, have your profession represented in them. How do you feel about this idea now? That changes everything, doesn’t it? When you look to your left and you look to your right and you think this guy is going to a meeting tomorrow with a bunch of BNI people and are making the same promises and commitment that they are making here today, guess what. No they are not. They are going to split the amount of referrals, which by the way, was the issue.

Two things were happening. They would either split the referrals and give half to one and half to the other, which isn’t bad- or they would do something worse. They would give both to both. Can you imagine getting a referral from somebody and they are also giving that referral to somebody else in another BNI group? That is the reason why years ago, the board of advisers said that you can’t be in two BNI groups. And a few years later, they said it’s really no different when you’re in another group just like BNI.

It’s a tough policy. In previous podcasts, we have talked about accountability. This is one example of it. Is it easy? No. Is it the right thing to do for the good of the whole group? Yes. It might not be the right thing for an individual person who would like to be in 3,4,5, or 6 groups because that would be a lot of business- and they would give everyone else some business and spread it around, but at a very small percentage.

So the only person it would benefit is the person who is in multiple groups. The whole does not benefit. That is when two is not better than one.

Priscilla:
Okay, now I am going to interrupt and ask a question. I know how some people get around this. They have a partner in their business and the partner is in another group. What do you think about that?

Ivan:
That’s interesting. I’m not sure I would call that getting around it. That came up a lot in the initial discussion. I am married and have a wife. I’ve been married 23 years. She has a whole different set of contacts than I have. Now we are talking about partners who are generally not spouses, but sometimes are. Those partners have a whole different set of contacts. So the discussion was about two different people with two separate sets of contacts. Do some of those contacts overlap Yes. But do all of those contacts overlap? Absolutely not. You can make that argument not just with a partner but with a spouse.

All I have to do is walk in the neighborhood. If I am walking in the neighborhood with Elisabeth, everyone is like, “Hi Elisabeth!” and “It’s good to see you, Elisabeth.” I’m like, you know those people? Oh yeah, they are the neighbor or they live a few blocks down. So she has contacts that I don’t have. I have contacts that she doesn’t have.

Priscilla:
So you think that’s okay.

Ivan:
Yeah, the board thought that was okay because you have two individuals with two sets of contacts. But one person has the same contacts and having them go to two different groups is going to substantially reduce the amount of referrals. Plus, a partner may have a referral that they would give without ever talking to the other partner. So yes, a partner is acceptable but the same person is not.

Priscilla:
Okay. That makes sense.

Ivan:
It was a good question.

Priscilla:
Well good. I think that makes sense. Is there anything else that you want to share with us?

Ivan:
No, I think that is it for today. I know this is a controversial topic. I would ask the BNI members- I always love to get constructive comments on the podcast. We get mostly constructive comments on the podcast. I would love to have people who think about having someone sitting next to you on your left and right and knowing that they belong to another BNI group. How would that make you feel? IT makes sense then that you can’t be in two BNI groups. We learned that years ago. And there’s no difference in being in BNI and a group like BNI.

It’s the hard thing to do but it’s the right thing to do. Don’t let someone talk you into mediocrity when excellence is an option. That is all I have for today, Priscilla. Thank you very much.

Priscilla:
You’re welcome. Thank you. That’s it for this week. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by networkingnow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

Posted in Building Better BNI Chapters, History of BNI

You may also like...

« Episode 249: The Four Pillars of a Successful BNI Group
Episode 251: Unsolicited Advice Is Rarely Appreciated »

Comments

    Solutions-focused comments are welcome. Dr. Misner reads every comment personally.

    All comments are moderated before posting. Profanity, personal attacks, and sales pitches will be deleted.

  1. Dan Bouchard says

    April 4, 2012 at 9:14 am

    In our area there is really only one other referral group like BNI. I hear all the time that “they” allow a member to belong to both their group “and” BNI. I find this amusing because the truth is, they are simply allowing BNI to enforce a rule that they agree with, but don’t want to enforce.
    So if I belong to XYZ group, and I want to join BNI, XYZ will say: “Sure, no Problem”, knowing full well that BNI won’t allow it. Problem solved.
    One other comment about partners belonging to different BNIs. I’m lucky because in my group I have someone who has a partner in a different group and that group doesn’t have anyone in my profession. So I get referrals from both partners. That’s the upside.

    Reply

  2. Jessica Swearingen says

    April 4, 2012 at 11:55 am

    I enjoy this podcast. Within our group I enjoy getting to know each and every member and the way their business works. If I was involved with two groups I feel like I would be overwhelmed and not honest with the members of the group.

    Reply

  3. Royal Fraser says

    April 4, 2012 at 2:18 pm

    Hello Ivan,

    It was a real treat to meet you last Thursday, here in San Diego, at the “Business Networking And Sex” event. You and I talked about having lots of request for photos while being in India and your family visit to the Whaley House. I hope you had a good break.

    Thanks for this weeks podcast, very helpful when explaining this policy to prospective members at the visitors meeting. I gave a brief talk today at our BNI meeting of Partners in Growth about the diffences between how men and women network. I really got their attention when I said that women spend less time networking and get better results.

    Best,
    -Royal

    Reply

  4. LAURA says

    April 5, 2012 at 2:37 pm

    I see the logic, but why is it ok that in our chapter we have one company who has two different employees belong to two different chapters, it seems like that is still a split loyalty

    Reply

  5. James Hance says

    April 6, 2012 at 12:32 pm

    Loyalty is the secret to BNI success. When members of your chapter see your loyalty, they will want to give you business referrals. When you do not demonstrate loyalty, you are just another person pitching for more business.

    Do what’s right for your fellow chapter members, and enjoy the benefits of belonging to a BNI chapter.

    Reply

  6. David Demangos says

    April 7, 2012 at 3:51 pm

    Two strong contact network groups doesn’t work. Splitting committments will decrease your credibility. Be accountable, one strong contact group, a chamber, and a casual group works well, otherwise you stretch yourself too thin and you will end up being average…or below.

    Reply

  7. Gloria Justice says

    April 8, 2012 at 7:51 pm

    This is a great explanation of a policy that initially didn’t seem to make sense. Thank you!

    Reply

  8. Bob says

    April 9, 2012 at 2:02 pm

    To David Demangos – I agree with you, but would be interested to see how you define a “casual” group. How different is it from a bonafide BNI group?

    Reply

  9. Ivan Misner says

    April 9, 2012 at 2:52 pm

    Bob, I talk about “casual contact” networks in Networking Like a Pro and The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret. They are groups like a Chamber of Commerce.

    Reply

  10. Eileen Hall says

    April 16, 2012 at 8:04 pm

    My first experience in BNI, I ran into this issue in my chapter all too soon. A few members were unabashedly trying to recruit me to their other BNI-like networking group. I got the impression that no one thought it was a big deal. Yet, I didn’t like the idea, for the exact reasons you describe and the real potential for diluted referrals and split allegiances. Thank you for reminding me that these rules do exist and we do have recourse. I always bring up the point that if you are really working hard in your chapter and doing everything you can to give qualified referrals, you really shouldn’t have time or energy to network with a second group. Well, maybe you could if it’s a whole different thing, like a charity group or chamber or other service organization.

    Reply

  11. John says

    May 14, 2012 at 9:30 am

    Ivan,

    I’m a member of a great BNI Chapter, am very enthusiastic about BNI and serve on the Membership Committee. I’ve got a business opportunity that I feel might conflict with BNI but, at the same time, I feel I might be able to pass more referrals to BNI because of it. Here it is:

    I am in discussion with some fellow marketing professionals about forming a creative cooperative. This cooperative will give us better visibility and credibility by establishing a proximity to other professionals. There will be no requirement to pass business within the co-op, but it is understood that business will be passed, by the nature of the structure.

    This, in my mind, is distinctly different than BNI and would even be a benefit for my BNI Chapter members because my business would be exposed to a greater volume of higher level clients – thus creating more referral opportunities.

    Now, what do you think?

    Thanks in advance.

    Reply

  12. Ivan Misner says

    May 14, 2012 at 10:01 am

    Hello John. You ask a good question.

    First, let me give you my “impression,” and then let me tell you BNI’s policy.

    My impression is that this doesn’t sound like a conflict because the group doesn’t meet every week and its purpose isn’t to pass referrals. However, the devil is always in the details. Which leads me to our policy. The Membership Committee is the body in a chapter that is charged with making sure there are no conflicts. You might want to run this by them.

    Hope this helps.

    Ivan

    Reply

  13. John Paul Henry says

    May 14, 2012 at 1:41 pm

    Thanks again for the timely response. If I decide to proceed with the opportunity I’ll follow up with you about the Membership Committee’s discussion on the matter.

    Keep up the great work.

    Reply

  14. Jennufer Scarponi says

    August 14, 2012 at 5:54 pm

    I agree with Ivan. It is a conflict of interest, and makes it impossible to really become close to a group.

    Reply

  15. N. E. Tucker says

    September 9, 2013 at 8:57 am

    Wish I had known this podcast existed all the many times I have tried to answer this question. Ivan did it well! Going forward I will refer those who ask about the policy to podcast #250. However, I do have one detail to address: the phrase “just like BNI”. While there are other organizations that limit membership to one-per-category, none others are at the professional standard or benefit level of BNI. Maybe they have a characteristic in common with BNI. But “just like” ???? Not so sure ….

    Reply

  16. Anna Priester says

    January 21, 2014 at 11:31 am

    I think we should not allow more than one member from a single company. It dilutes the value of those members to their respective chapters and is the wrong way to build chapter numbers!!

    Reply

  17. David Stevens says

    March 26, 2014 at 4:23 pm

    I agree it should be one Member per company. In most cases there is one decision maker. They will decide who gets referred business. If there are competing companies in the other groups vying for their business, but all doing the same type of business, usually only the Chapter with the controlling member gets the business. If this were the rule sole proprietors would be on the same level playing field as those that have 25 employees and it would open up the other Chapters to additional members from that field. A good portion of BNI chapters are made up of sole proprietors. The rules should be the same for all. One person from each company in BNI. If that wasn’t the case then the idea of others from that company being members of another networking group holds the same truths as you and your wife walking down the street. The other employees have different sets of contacts. Seems like what’s good for the Goose should be good for the Gander.

    Reply

  18. Kenn Burns says

    January 8, 2015 at 11:29 am

    There is a debate in my chapter right now on the meaning of the policy as it is worded. There is another networking that is not perceived by the chapter members as a threat, which does encourage referrals in exchange for a system of commissions, but it does not allow exclusivity in your category. (ie there are several real estate agents, insurance brokers, etc)

    In applying the policy, which I believe today is #4 on the list, it says members “cannot be a member of any other group that allows only one person per profession and whose primary purpose is to pass referrals to one another”. In reading this, the debate we are having is 1) does the group have to meet both conditions, exclusivity and referrals, to be considered against policy or 2) does the group have to meet only one of these conditions, exclusivity or referrals, to be considered against policy?

    Reply

  19. Ivan Misner says

    February 4, 2015 at 12:51 pm

    Ken, you have a good question. The devil is always in the details. I’d ask that you bring your local BNI Director into this discussion by providing all the information you can. If your Director needs assistance, have him or her contact Suzi, my Customer Care supervisor at BNI HQ.

    Thanks.

    Ivan

    Reply

  20. Steven Lutch says

    October 28, 2015 at 1:40 pm

    Good points all around. Steve L

    Reply

  21. Sophie says

    October 28, 2015 at 6:22 pm

    It makes sense for a company of two or more staff to belong to different BNIs, but not one person belonging to many. The splitting of referrals can be frustrating. There are many BNIs i have visited and they dont have my profession represented. I’m waiting to expand my team and send them over.

    Reply

  22. Eric Palmer says

    November 4, 2015 at 10:20 am

    I appreciate this podcast. It clears up question that have come up in my chapter.

    Reply

  23. anon says

    August 26, 2019 at 1:48 pm

    We had a similar but unique situation in our group; two partners, the CEO and CMO, that wanted to attend our meetings. The CEO was going to apply and have the CMO tag along. I thought this would be great. We’d have two “contact lists” from one company in the room. Our president opposed the idea which led to neither the CEO nor the CMO turning in an application.

    What are the rules on one company having more than one employee attend meetings?

    Reply

    • Ivan Misner says

      August 28, 2019 at 1:45 pm

      Generally two people from the same company in the same category is not a problem. HOWEVER, the devil is always in the details and a post from “Anon” is a flag to me. Talk to your local BNI Director and feel free to have them reach out to me.

      Reply

      • Anon says

        August 28, 2019 at 2:12 pm

        Anon was to be respectful of our president and not throw anyone under the bus in a public forum

      • Ivan Misner says

        August 28, 2019 at 2:59 pm

        Understood, I appreciate that. My experience has been that there are often many intervening variables that aren’t mentioned and that “flavored” my response. That said, I think it’s important to talk to the local BNI Director.

  24. SD says

    August 28, 2019 at 2:38 pm

    Good for you, “Anon”!
    Refreshing to see civility and caring for your administrative team.

    Reply
